title: Sleepless Nights
description: Your baby has been waking up every two hours crying. You're exhausted, and it's affecting your work performance. How do you handle the sleep deprivation?

choices.text: Power through it — be there every time they cry
choices.result_text: You're running on fumes, but your baby feels safe and secure knowing you're always there.

choices.text: Try sleep training — let them cry it out sometimes
choices.result_text: After a few tough nights, the baby starts sleeping longer. You feel more rested, though the crying was hard to ignore.

choices.text: Ask family for help with night shifts
choices.result_text: Your family steps in to help. The baby bonds with multiple caregivers, and you finally get some rest.

title: First Words
description: Your toddler is starting to babble and form sounds. You notice they respond differently to various types of interaction. How do you encourage their language development?

choices.text: Read to them every single day, multiple times
choices.result_text: The daily reading ritual becomes a cherished bonding time. Your child's vocabulary grows noticeably faster than peers.

choices.text: Use educational screen time and apps
choices.result_text: The colorful apps hold their attention, and they pick up some words. But you notice they get fussy when the screen goes away.

choices.text: Talk to them constantly during daily activities
choices.result_text: Narrating your day becomes second nature. Your child starts pointing at things and attempting words with enthusiasm.

title: Daycare Decision
description: Parental leave is ending. You need to decide on childcare arrangements. Each option has tradeoffs for your family's finances and your child's development.

choices.text: Enroll in a quality daycare center ($1200/month)
choices.result_text: The daycare is excellent but expensive. Your child thrives socially but sometimes seems overwhelmed by the busy environment.

choices.text: Find a home daycare provider ($700/month)
choices.result_text: The smaller setting suits your child well. They form a close bond with the caregiver and a few other children.

choices.text: Reduce work hours and care for them yourself
choices.result_text: The financial hit is real, but the bond you're building is irreplaceable. You're tired but present for every milestone.

title: Tantrum in Public
description: Your toddler throws a massive tantrum in the grocery store. They're screaming, kicking, and people are staring. How do you respond?

choices.text: Calmly get down to their level and acknowledge their feelings
choices.result_text: It takes time, but your child eventually calms down. They're learning that big feelings are okay and that you're a safe person to have them around.

choices.text: Firmly say no and remove them from the store immediately
choices.result_text: Your child learns there are consequences. The tantrums become less frequent in stores, though they still happen at home.

choices.text: Give them what they want to stop the scene
choices.result_text: Peace is restored immediately, but you notice the tantrums are getting more frequent. They've learned that screaming works.

choices.text: Raise your voice and threaten punishment
choices.result_text: The tantrum stops from shock, but your child looks frightened. Other parents give you disapproving looks. You feel guilty later.

title: Picky Eater
description: Your child refuses to eat anything that isn't chicken nuggets or plain pasta. Mealtimes have become a daily battle. What's your approach?

choices.text: Keep offering variety — they eat what's served or nothing
choices.result_text: It's a long battle of wills, but gradually your child starts trying new foods. Some nights they go to bed hungry, but their palate is expanding.

choices.text: Let them eat what they want — they'll grow out of it
choices.result_text: Mealtimes are peaceful, but the pediatrician mentions some nutritional concerns at the next checkup.

choices.text: Make it fun — let them help cook and choose ingredients
choices.result_text: Your kitchen is messier, but your child takes pride in 'their' creations and is more willing to taste things they helped make.

title: Separation Anxiety
description: Every time you leave the room, your toddler screams and clings to you desperately. Drop-offs at daycare have become traumatic for both of you.

choices.text: Practice short separations and always come back when promised
choices.result_text: Slowly, trust builds. Your child learns that you always come back. The goodbyes get easier over weeks.

choices.text: Sneak away when they're distracted to avoid the tears
choices.result_text: The immediate crying stops, but your child becomes hypervigilant. They watch you constantly, afraid you'll disappear again.

choices.text: Stay home more — they clearly need you right now
choices.result_text: Your child feels secure, but you're sacrificing your own needs. The dependency seems to be growing rather than fading.

title: Playground Conflict
description: At the playground, another child pushes yours off the slide. Your child is crying and looking to you for help. The other parent isn't paying attention.

choices.text: Comfort your child and help them use words to express their feelings
choices.result_text: Your child learns that words are more powerful than fists. They start saying 'that hurt my feelings' to other kids.

choices.text: March over and confront the other parent
choices.result_text: The confrontation is awkward but your child sees you standing up for them. They feel protected, but also learn that conflict is solved with aggression.

choices.text: Tell your child to stand up for themselves next time
choices.result_text: Your child starts pushing back when pushed. They're less of a target, but the daycare mentions some 'rough play' incidents.

title: Potty Training Struggles
description: Your child is 3 and still not potty trained. Preschool requires it for enrollment. Every attempt has ended in frustration for both of you.

choices.text: Create a reward chart with stickers and small prizes
choices.result_text: The reward system clicks! Your child is motivated by the stickers and masters it within a few weeks. They're proud of themselves.

choices.text: Wait — they'll do it when they're ready
choices.result_text: A few more months of diapers, but when your child finally decides they're ready, it happens almost overnight with minimal stress.

choices.text: Set a strict schedule — bathroom trips every hour, no exceptions
choices.result_text: The rigid schedule works mechanically, but your child seems anxious about accidents. They're trained but not confident about it.

title: Screen Time Debate
description: Your partner (or family member) thinks some TV is fine for the toddler. You've read that screens before age 2 can affect development. The baby is fussy and the screen calms them instantly.

choices.text: Strict no-screen policy until age 2
choices.result_text: It's harder without the 'electronic babysitter,' but your child develops strong imaginative play skills. Family members think you're too strict.

choices.text: Limited educational content — 30 minutes max
choices.result_text: A balanced approach. Your child enjoys their shows but transitions away without too much fuss. You get a brief daily break.

choices.text: Use it freely as a tool — whatever keeps the peace
choices.result_text: The house is calmer, but your child is increasingly dependent on screens. They throw fits when the tablet is taken away.

title: Medical Scare
description: Your baby spikes a high fever in the middle of the night. They're lethargic and you're terrified. The ER wait is 4 hours, or you could try to manage it at home.

choices.text: Rush to the emergency room immediately
choices.result_text: It turns out to be a common virus. The ER bill stings, but you have peace of mind. Better safe than sorry with a baby.

choices.text: Call the nurse hotline and follow their guidance at home
choices.result_text: The nurse talks you through fever management. By morning, the fever breaks. You feel more confident handling these situations.

choices.text: Give infant Tylenol and monitor through the night
choices.result_text: A long, anxious night. The fever eventually breaks but you wonder if you should have sought help sooner.

title: Biting Phase
description: The daycare calls — your child has been biting other kids. This is the third incident this week. They're threatening to remove your child from the program.

choices.text: Work intensively on teaching 'gentle hands' and emotional vocabulary
choices.result_text: It takes weeks of consistent effort, but the biting stops. Your child starts using words like 'frustrated' and 'angry' instead.

choices.text: Punish firmly each time — immediate time-out, no exceptions
choices.result_text: The biting decreases quickly due to fear of punishment. But the underlying frustration hasn't been addressed.

choices.text: Pull them from daycare and find a smaller setting
choices.result_text: In the smaller group, your child is calmer and the biting stops. But they're getting less social practice.

title: New Sibling Question
description: Your child asks why they don't have a brother or sister like their friends. You and your partner have been discussing this. The timing isn't ideal financially.

choices.text: Explain that your family is perfect as it is
choices.result_text: Your child seems satisfied with the answer. They enjoy being the center of attention and develop strong independent play skills.

choices.text: Promise to think about it — maybe someday
choices.result_text: Your child brings it up occasionally but accepts the vague answer. The question lingers in your own mind though.

choices.text: Start trying — a sibling would be good for them
choices.result_text: Months later, a new baby arrives. Your firstborn is excited but also jealous. Life gets much more complicated — and expensive.

title: Imaginary Friend
description: Your 3-year-old has developed an imaginary friend named 'Boo' who they talk to constantly. They insist on setting a place for Boo at dinner. Is this normal or concerning?

choices.text: Play along enthusiastically — set the place, ask about Boo
choices.result_text: Boo becomes part of the family lore. Your child's imagination flourishes, and they develop complex storytelling abilities.

choices.text: Gently redirect — 'Boo is pretend, let's focus on real friends'
choices.result_text: Your child seems a little deflated but starts seeking out more real-world social interactions. Boo fades away over time.

choices.text: Consult the pediatrician — this seems excessive
choices.result_text: The doctor assures you it's perfectly normal and actually a sign of healthy cognitive development. You feel relieved but slightly embarrassed.

title: Preschool Interview
description: The best preschool in your area has a competitive application process. Your child would need to 'interview' (play session observed by teachers). It's expensive but highly rated.

choices.text: Apply and prep your child with practice social scenarios
choices.result_text: Your child gets in! The program is excellent, with small classes and engaged teachers. The cost is significant but the quality shows.

choices.text: Choose the affordable neighborhood preschool instead
choices.result_text: It's basic but adequate. Your child makes friends and learns the basics. The money saved reduces household stress.

choices.text: Skip preschool — teach them at home until kindergarten
choices.result_text: Your child is academically ahead but socially behind when kindergarten arrives. They know their letters but struggle with sharing.

title: The 'Why' Phase
description: Your child has entered the endless 'why?' phase. Every answer leads to another 'but why?' It's relentless — from morning until bedtime. Your patience is being tested.

choices.text: Answer every question thoughtfully, no matter how tired you are
choices.result_text: Your child develops an insatiable love of learning. They start making connections between things that surprise you. It's exhausting but rewarding.

choices.text: Turn it back on them — 'What do YOU think?'
choices.result_text: Your child starts forming their own theories about the world. Some are hilariously wrong, but the critical thinking is developing beautifully.

choices.text: Sometimes just say 'because that's how it is' — you need a break
choices.result_text: Your child learns that not every question gets answered. They stop asking you and start figuring things out on their own — or just accepting uncertainty.

title: First Friendship
description: Your child has formed their first real friendship at the park. They talk about 'their best friend' constantly. But the other child's family has very different values and parenting styles from yours.

choices.text: Encourage the friendship — different perspectives are healthy
choices.result_text: Your child learns to navigate different family cultures. They become more adaptable and understanding of differences.

choices.text: Supervise closely and limit unsupervised playtime
choices.result_text: The friendship continues under your watchful eye. Your child doesn't pick up any concerning behaviors, but the other family notices your hovering.

choices.text: Gently redirect toward other children you approve of more
choices.result_text: Your child is confused about why they can't see their friend anymore. They make new connections but something feels controlled.

title: First Smile
description: Your baby just gave you their first real smile — not gas, an actual smile. It melts your heart after weeks of exhaustion. How do you respond to this milestone?

choices.text: Drop everything and spend the whole evening playing with them
choices.result_text: You spent hours making silly faces, and each smile felt like a reward. Your bond deepens.
choices.timeline_text: Celebrated first smile with dedicated playtime

choices.text: Take a photo and share it with family
choices.result_text: The photo gets dozens of likes and loving comments. Your family feels connected to the baby's growth.
choices.timeline_text: Shared first smile with extended family

choices.text: Note it in a baby journal and keep going with your routine
choices.result_text: You document the milestone carefully. Structure and routine keep things running smoothly.
choices.timeline_text: Documented first smile in baby journal

title: Feeding Struggles
description: Your baby is having trouble feeding — whether breast or bottle, mealtimes have become stressful battles. The pediatrician says the baby is slightly underweight.

choices.text: Hire a lactation consultant / feeding specialist
choices.result_text: The specialist identifies the issue quickly. Feedings become easier, and the baby starts gaining weight.
choices.timeline_text: Hired feeding specialist — baby's health improved

choices.text: Research solutions online and try different approaches
choices.result_text: After trying several techniques, you find one that works. It took time, but you figured it out.

choices.text: Switch to formula / a different formula brand
choices.result_text: The new formula agrees with the baby much better. Weight gain normalizes within weeks.

title: Postpartum Adjustment
description: The reality of parenthood is hitting hard. You're questioning whether you're doing this right. A friend suggests joining a parent support group.

choices.text: Join the support group — you need other parents to talk to
choices.result_text: The group becomes a lifeline. Hearing other parents' struggles makes you feel less alone.

choices.text: Push through alone — you can handle this
choices.result_text: You tough it out. Some days are brutal, but you're building inner strength.
choices.timeline_text: Chose to handle early parenthood independently

choices.text: Talk to your doctor about how you're feeling
choices.result_text: Your doctor provides helpful resources and reassurance. Taking care of yourself helps you take care of your baby.
choices.timeline_text: Sought medical support for postpartum adjustment

title: Visitor Overload
description: Everyone wants to meet the new baby. Family and friends are showing up constantly, and your tiny apartment feels crowded. The baby seems overstimulated.

choices.text: Set visiting hours and enforce boundaries
choices.result_text: Some family members are miffed, but the baby sleeps better and you feel more in control.

choices.text: Welcome everyone — the baby needs to know their family
choices.result_text: The baby gets lots of attention and stimulation. You're exhausted but the family bonds are strong.
choices.timeline_text: Welcomed all visitors to meet the baby

choices.text: Ask visitors to help with chores while they're here
choices.result_text: Visitors are happy to help, and you get some much-needed rest while the baby socializes.
choices.timeline_text: Put visitors to work helping with the household

title: Baby Won't Stop Crying
description: It's been three hours of non-stop crying. You've tried everything — feeding, changing, rocking, singing. Nothing works. You're at your breaking point.

choices.text: Put the baby safely in the crib and take a 10-minute break
choices.result_text: You step outside, breathe, and come back calmer. Sometimes the baby just needs to cry it out, and you need to stay sane.
choices.timeline_text: Took a needed break during crying episode

choices.text: Keep trying — drive around the block, try white noise, anything
choices.result_text: After another hour of attempts, the car ride finally works. You're completely drained but your baby knows you never gave up.

choices.text: Call your mother / a trusted family member for help
choices.result_text: Grandma arrives and somehow the baby calms down immediately. It takes a village.
choices.timeline_text: Called family for help with inconsolable baby

title: Work or Stay Home?
description: Your parental leave is ending soon. You need to decide: go back to work full-time, try to negotiate part-time, or find a way to stay home longer.

choices.text: Go back full-time — you need the income
choices.result_text: The paycheck helps, but leaving your baby at daycare every morning is harder than you expected.

choices.text: Negotiate part-time or remote work
choices.result_text: Your employer agrees to a flexible arrangement. Less money, but more time with your baby.

choices.text: Quit and stay home — these years matter most
choices.result_text: Money is tight, but you're there for every first. Every smile, every word, every step.
choices.timeline_text: Quit work to be a stay-at-home parent

choices.text: Find affordable daycare and go back to work
choices.result_text: The daycare is decent and your baby starts socializing with other children. But the cost is significant.
choices.timeline_text: Enrolled baby in daycare and returned to work
